A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Bonaventure

c. 1217 – 1274

The Franciscan mind on the journey of the soul into God.

The working thesis is this: Bonaventure’s philosophy of mind is an exemplarist, illuminationist, affective, embodied, and comparative noetics. The human mind is not merely an inner faculty for forming representations. It is the rational soul as an embodied image of God: it receives the world through sensible likenesses, abstracts intelligibles by its own agent intellect, judges with certitude under the divine light, knows itself through memory–intelligence–will, is either distorted or healed by love, and reaches its summit in a kind of experiential knowing that exceeds discursive thought without simply abolishing intellect. The “mind of God” and the minds of angels are not detachable theological ornaments in this system. They are the two boundary cases that allow Bonaventure to articulate what finite created mind is: God as the uncreated source of intelligibility, truth, and love; angels as finite incorporeal intellectual creatures whose cognition clarifies both the dignity and limits of human cognition.

Bonaventure did not write a modern “philosophy of mind” treatise. He wrote as a theologian, metaphysician, biblical exegete, Franciscan master, and spiritual author. Still, he explicitly distinguishes philosophy and theology, treating philosophy as a rational light and a real mode of truth even though it is subordinate to theology in the final architectonic order. That means a philosophy of mind can be extracted from him, but only if we do not falsify the material by stripping away illumination, Christology, Trinitarian analogy, grace, angelic intellect, or mystical experience as allegedly “non-philosophical” residues.

1. Orientation: what “mind” means in Bonaventure

The main Latin terms matter. Anima is the soul in its broad sense: the principle of life, including vegetative, sensitive, and rational operations. Mens is more specific: the higher rational interiority of the soul, especially as it bears the image of God. Spiritus may refer to the soul as spiritual and inwardly turned toward God. Intellectus, intelligentia, and ratio name intellectual powers and modes of understanding. Memoria, intelligentia, and voluntas form the Augustinian triad by which the soul is an image of the Trinity. Affectus, amor, and voluntas name the affective-volitional side of mind, which is not secondary for Bonaventure. Synderesis, apex mentis, or apex affectus designate the highest affective point or summit of the soul in certain contexts. Species, similitudo, vestigium, and imago belong to his theory of representation, signification, and participation. Lumen, ars aeterna, and Verbum name the light, divine art, and Word through which truth is ultimately grounded.

So “mind” in Bonaventure is not Cartesian consciousness sealed inside a private interior chamber. It is the rational soul considered as a living, embodied, intentional, self-reflexive, morally ordered, God-imaging power. Mind is cognitive, but never merely cognitive. It is also affective, volitional, appetitive, contemplative, and liturgical in the broad medieval sense: it is ordered toward truth, goodness, beatitude, and praise.

Bonaventure’s system is architectonic. The First Principle is the origin of creatures; creatures proceed from God through exemplar causality; and the soul’s final task is return, reformation, and union. The human mind apprehends the physical world, but Bonaventure is not an empiricist in the modern sense: sensation, abstraction, illumination, moral purification, and contemplative ascent are all parts of one larger account of knowing.

2. The metaphysical background: exemplarism and the hierarchy of minds

The central metaphysical fact behind Bonaventure’s philosophy of mind is that creatures are intelligible because they are made according to divine exemplars. Bonaventure is a Christian Neoplatonist in the broad Augustinian-Franciscan line: beings proceed from God, bear traces of their source, and can lead the mind back to that source. The created world is not brute material fact. It is structured, meaningful, and knowable because it is caused by, ordered to, and patterned upon divine Wisdom.

This is why exemplar causality is indispensable. Efficient causality explains that things come from God; final causality explains that they are ordered back to God; exemplar causality explains why they are intelligible at all. Bonaventure’s metaphysics repeatedly frames creation in terms of divine causality and exemplarity, and modern scholarship rightly treats exemplar causality as one of the grand themes of his philosophy.

The mind exists inside this hierarchy. There is, first, the divine intellect or divine Wisdom, understood with care as uncreated, simple, infinite, and identical with God’s being. There are, second, angelic minds: finite incorporeal intellectual creatures. There are, third, human minds: finite rational souls embodied in matter, dependent on sensation in their present condition, but capable of intellectual knowledge, self-knowledge, moral freedom, and union with God. Bonaventure’s metaphysics therefore invites a comparative philosophy of mind: divine mind, angelic mind, and human mind are not three species within a neutral genus called “mind,” but they are hierarchically related modes of intellectual life.

Bonaventure’s universal hylomorphism is relevant here. Against the view that only bodily creatures are composed of matter and form, Bonaventure holds that every created being with potency, including angels, has a matter-form composition in some sense. God alone is pure act. This is not a crude materialization of angels; rather, it is a way of marking the ontological difference between finite created intellect and the uncreated divine intellect.

The result is a philosophy in which mentality is never free-floating. A mind is intelligible only by its place in the order of being. Divine mind is source; angelic mind is incorporeal created intellect; human mind is embodied created intellect; animal cognition is sensitive but not rational; and material creatures are vestiges rather than images of God.

3. The human soul: embodied microcosm and spiritual image

Bonaventure’s human soul is both embodied and spiritual. The human person is a microcosm, standing at the intersection of the material and spiritual orders. In the Itinerarium mentis in Deum, Bonaventure says that the sensible world enters the soul through the five senses as through doors. Cognition begins not with a disembodied intuition but with the reception of likenesses or species through bodily sense organs, media, and internal powers.

This means that embodiment is not an accidental inconvenience for human cognition. In the present life, human knowing normally begins from sensible forms. The body mediates access to the world. The senses are doors, not prisons. Yet the soul is not reducible to those doors, because the forms received through sense are purified, abstracted, judged, and finally referred beyond themselves.

The human soul is also the image of God, especially in its higher triadic structure of memory, intelligence, and will. Bonaventure’s account is deeply Augustinian: the mind remembers, understands, and loves; and in this triadic unity it mirrors, analogically, the Trinity. Bonaventure insists that the mind loves, knows, and remembers itself, and that this self-relation is central to its status as imago Dei.

A reconstructed Bonaventurian philosophy of mind must therefore hold two claims together. First, ordinary human cognition is embodied, sensory, and mediated. Second, the rational soul is spiritual, self-reflexive, and ordered to eternal truth. Bonaventure is neither a reductive materialist nor a dualist who treats bodily life as irrelevant to cognition. He gives us something closer to a sacramental or participatory account of embodied mind: sensible cognition is real, but it is ordered toward intellectual, moral, and contemplative fulfillment.

4. Perception: species, similitudes, and intentionality

Bonaventure’s theory of perception turns on species or similitudes. Sensible cognition does not occur by the physical substance of the thing entering the soul. Rather, the thing generates a likeness, which is transmitted through a medium, received by the sense organ, and carried into the internal sensory power. This is a medieval intentional theory of perception: the object becomes present to the knower by a form or likeness, not by material transfer.

He analyzes this process in three stages: apprehension, delight, and judgment. Apprehension is the initial reception of the sensible likeness. Delight arises when the perceived object is experienced as fitting, beautiful, proportionate, or wholesome. Judgment is the higher act in which the soul evaluates and abstracts from the merely particular circumstances of place, time, and motion.

This is philosophically significant. Perception is not bare registration of sensory data. It already contains affective and evaluative dimensions. Beauty, proportion, and fittingness are not late subjective projections added after cognition; they are part of the way the world is cognitively encountered. Bonaventure’s account thus resists a sharp modern division between perception, affect, and judgment.

The perceived species also has a symbolic and theological function. A likeness leads the soul back to the object of which it is a likeness, and ultimately to the source of all likeness, the divine Word. For Bonaventure, sensible cognition can become contemplative because the world is not mute. It is a field of vestiges. Created things communicate something of their source, not because every creature is itself a rational sign-user, but because each creature bears an intelligible form derived from the Eternal Art.

A modern analogy would be this: Bonaventure’s perception theory is both intentionalist and semiotic. It is intentionalist because cognition is directed to objects through forms or likenesses. It is semiotic because those likenesses participate in a broader order of signs that can lead the mind from creature to Creator. The analogy is helpful, but it should not be flattened into modern representationalism. Bonaventure’s species are not private mental pictures; they are mediating likenesses within a metaphysical order of participation.

5. Abstraction and the agent intellect

A common mistake is to make Bonaventure an anti-Aristotelian who rejects abstraction in favor of illumination. That is too crude. Bonaventure accepts a real role for abstraction. The intellect has an active power that abstracts intelligible forms from sensible material, and a passive or possible power that receives intelligible forms. The active and possible intellect are not two separate substances; they are powers within the same rational soul.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Bonaventure preserves the activity of the created knower. The human mind is not a passive screen on which God projects truths. Nor is the agent intellect simply God acting in the soul. The Spanish reception is useful here: Víctor Muñiz Rodríguez’s study of illumination and the agent intellect emphasizes that illumination is a metaphysical doctrine and that the agent intellect is not God but a light of the human soul.

So Bonaventure’s noetics is cooperative. The created mind works. Sense supplies material; imagination and internal powers mediate; the agent intellect abstracts; the possible intellect receives; judgment evaluates. Divine illumination does not replace these operations. It grounds the certainty and normativity of some of them.

This point is crucial for extracting a philosophy of mind. Bonaventure is not an occasionalist about cognition. He does not say that God knows instead of us. The created mind has its own intrinsic powers. But those powers do not fully explain the mind’s access to necessary, immutable, and certain truth.

6. Divine illumination: not a theological distraction, but Bonaventure’s theory of cognitive normativity

Bonaventure’s doctrine of illumination is one of the central pieces of his philosophy of mind. It answers a specific question: how can a mutable human mind, knowing mutable created objects, attain certainty about universal, necessary, and immutable truths?

His answer is moderate illumination. The mind receives content from created things and abstracts intelligible forms through its own powers, but certitude requires some relation to an immutable light. Bonaventure does not claim that God is the sole cause of knowledge, nor that the human mind directly sees the divine ideas with complete clarity in ordinary cognition. Rather, the divine light functions as a regulative and motivating ground of certainty. Created causes provide abstraction, universality, and correspondence; illumination accounts for the necessity and certitude of judgment.

The broader history of divine illumination supports this reading. The doctrine was developed by Augustine and later systematized by thirteenth-century Franciscans, including Bonaventure and Matthew of Aquasparta. The classic argument rests on the changeability of both the human mind and the created world: certain knowledge seems to require an immutable source of truth.

The key point is that illumination need not be understood as God inserting new information into the mind. It can be understood as providing insight, warrant, or justification: the mind sees the truth of a proposition or principle under a light that it does not generate by itself. This is why contemporary discussions of illumination often distinguish between receiving information and receiving the conditions for recognizing truth.

In modern terms, Bonaventure’s illumination theory is partly an account of epistemic externalism and partly a transcendental account of cognitive normativity. The mind’s certainty depends on something outside the finite cognitive system, but not in a way that cancels the system’s own operations. The human intellect is active, but it is not self-grounding. It can judge truly because its acts are measured, implicitly or explicitly, by the Eternal Art.

This also explains why illumination belongs to philosophy of mind, not merely to devotional theology. It is Bonaventure’s answer to the problem of how finite cognition can be normatively answerable to necessary truth.

7. Memory: self-presence, temporality, and first principles

Bonaventure’s account of memory is much richer than the modern idea of memory as storage of past experiences. In Itinerarium III, memory retains past, present, and future; it also retains simple principles and eternal axioms. Memory is therefore a power of temporal synthesis, self-presence, and intellectual orientation toward eternity.

This is one of the places where Bonaventure most clearly follows Augustine. The soul’s memory is not merely empirical recall. It is the depth of the mind’s self-relation. Through memory the soul is present to itself; through memory it holds principles that are not reducible to sensory impressions; through memory it is opened to eternal truth.

This has direct implications for a Bonaventurian philosophy of mind. Self-consciousness is not first a detached act of introspective observation. It is rooted in the soul’s abiding self-presence. The mind remembers itself, understands itself, and loves itself. That self-presence is not absolute; it is finite, wounded, and often obscured. But it is constitutive of rational mind.

Memory also supports the mind’s relation to necessity. If the mind holds principles that cannot be derived merely from temporal flux, memory becomes a point of contact between finite consciousness and eternal truth. This does not mean that memory is divine. It means that memory, as a created power, is structured so as to receive and retain truths whose necessity exceeds the mutable mind.

8. Intelligence: definition, inference, and the presupposition of Being

Bonaventure describes intellect as working with terms, propositions, and inferences. Terms require definitions; definitions require higher and more general notions; and created beings are ultimately intelligible only through some relation to absolute Being. Propositions are known with certitude only under immutable light, and necessary inference points back to the archetypal order in the Eternal Art.

This gives us a strongly metaphysical account of conceptual thought. Concepts are not merely psychological classifications. To define a thing is to situate it in an intelligible order. To infer necessarily is to participate in an order of truth that the finite mind does not invent. To understand created being is, at least implicitly, to depend on a more ultimate notion of Being.

The result is not exactly an ontological argument at every moment of cognition, but it is a deep thesis about intelligibility: finite beings are understood against the horizon of Being, truth, and goodness. In Bonaventure’s mature thought, these transcendentals are not optional abstractions. They are the conditions under which created things are intelligible at all.

Here one can see why Bonaventure’s philosophy of mind cannot be cleanly separated from metaphysics. The intellect is not merely a natural faculty that processes data. It is a power whose acts presuppose an ontological order.

9. Will, affect, and the primacy of love

Bonaventure’s philosophy of mind is not intellectualist in a narrow sense. The will is not an afterthought, and love is not a mere emotional supplement to cognition. The elective power operates through deliberation, judgment, and desire. In choosing the better, the mind presupposes some orientation toward the best; in desiring, it is drawn toward the highest good.

Bonaventure’s moral psychology is therefore central to his noetics. Love is the first virtue and the form of the virtues; it orders the will toward the First Principle and transforms the whole soul. Moral reformation is not merely ethical behavior added to cognition from outside. It is the restoration of the mind’s capacity to see, judge, and desire rightly.

The German-language scholarship is helpful here. Nikolay Mladenov’s study emphasizes the special role of will and affect in Bonaventure’s account of cognition. He notes that the will can neglect or resist the judgment of intellect, that corruption of will affects memory and thought, and that grace restores cognitive powers. He also stresses that Bonaventure’s synderesis is not primarily an intellectual faculty but belongs to the volitional-affective side of the soul.

This is a major point for reconstruction. Bonaventure’s “mind” includes affective intentionality. The soul is not fully explained by perception plus concept formation plus inference. Love, desire, delight, and moral orientation are constitutive of how the mind relates to truth. A disordered will does not merely choose badly after the intellect has done its job; it can obstruct attention, distort judgment, and prevent the soul from returning to itself.

10. Cognitive pathology: distraction, phantasms, concupiscence

Bonaventure has a serious theory of cognitive failure. The mind is naturally near to God in the sense that it is made in God’s image and ordered toward divine truth, but it is distracted by cares, clouded by phantasms, and pulled away by concupiscence. These are not merely moral defects; they are noetic disorders. They prevent the soul from entering itself and recognizing the light by which it knows.

This yields a moral-epistemic model of attention. Vice is not only wrong willing; it is also a deformation of cognition. The soul that is dominated by disordered desire cannot see properly because it cannot attend properly. Conversely, grace and virtue do not merely make the person morally better; they restore the person’s cognitive and contemplative capacities.

For Bonaventure, therefore, epistemology and ascetic psychology are inseparable. The question “How do we know?” cannot be fully separated from “What kind of soul must one become in order to know rightly?” This is one of the strongest differences between Bonaventure and much modern epistemology.

11. The triadic structure of mind: memory, intelligence, will

The triad of memory, intelligence, and will is the core of Bonaventure’s account of the mind as image of God. From memory arises intelligence or the inner word; from memory and intelligence proceeds love. These powers are mutually immanent within the soul and together form the created analogue of Trinitarian life.

This analogy must be handled carefully. Bonaventure is not saying that the human mind is literally a Trinity. Nor is he reducing the Trinity to psychological faculties. The direction of explanation is theological and analogical: because the human mind is created in the image of God, its triadic structure can serve as a mirror through which the soul contemplates the divine Trinity.

The French reception is especially helpful on this point. A study of personhood in Bonaventure emphasizes that rational nature is the image of God through memory, intelligence, and will; that both angels and humans are images in this sense; and that Bonaventure differs from Aquinas by treating these faculties as deeply intrinsic to the soul rather than merely accidental powers added onto it.

For philosophy of mind, the key lesson is that mental unity is not monolithic. The mind is one substance or one rational soul, but its unity is internally articulated. It is self-retaining, self-expressing, and self-loving. Bonaventure thus gives us a model of mind as dynamic triadic unity rather than as a single undifferentiated consciousness.

12. The spiritual senses and non-discursive cognition

Bonaventure’s doctrine of the spiritual senses is another place where a modern interpreter may be tempted to dismiss material as purely mystical. That would be a mistake. For Bonaventure, the spiritual senses name modes of experiential cognition that correspond to the soul’s reformation. In Itinerarium IV, faith restores hearing and sight, hope restores smell, and charity restores taste and touch. These are not ordinary bodily sensations, but neither are they empty metaphors. They describe affective, experiential ways in which the soul comes to know divine reality.

Recent scholarship continues to treat this as an important feature of Bonaventure’s theory of knowledge. Júlia Puskás’s study of the spiritual senses traces the theme across the Commentary on the Sentences, De reductione artium ad theologiam, Breviloquium, and Itinerarium, emphasizing its relation to experiential knowledge of God, the hierarchy of spiritual perception, mystical excess, and Dionysian theology.

This gives Bonaventure a layered epistemology. There is sensory cognition. There is intellectual abstraction. There is judgment under illumination. There is self-knowledge through memory, intelligence, and will. There is affective-moral knowledge through love and virtue. And there is spiritual perception, in which the soul experiences divine reality through transformed affect.

This is not irrationalism. It is a claim that not all genuine knowledge is propositional or discursive. Bonaventure thinks some knowledge is experiential, participatory, and affective. The mind can know by being transformed, not merely by forming propositions.

13. Mystical excess: the limit of intellect, not its negation

The culmination of the Itinerarium is the famous seventh stage, where the mind passes beyond the sensible world and beyond itself. Bonaventure says that in the final passage intellectual operations are left behind and affection is transferred and transformed into God. He emphasizes unction over inquiry, gift over technique, and ecstatic passage over ordinary cognition.

This passage must be interpreted with precision. Bonaventure is not saying that intellect is worthless. The entire journey to that point has required perception, judgment, abstraction, self-knowledge, metaphysical reasoning, and contemplation. Nor is he saying that the soul becomes mindless. A good reading is that the intellect reaches a limit because its object exceeds its ordinary discursive capacities. The faculties remain, but the mode of union is experiential and trans-propositional rather than conceptual in the usual sense. Contemporary interpretation of Bonaventure’s mystical ascent often stresses exactly this: the faculties remain intact, but the object exceeds ordinary cognitive capability.

A Bonaventurian philosophy of mind therefore has a theory of cognitive limit. Discursive intellect is real and indispensable, but it is not the final form of knowing. The highest act is not a syllogism but loving union. That does not make it non-cognitive. It means cognition is fulfilled in a mode where truth is possessed as presence, not merely represented as proposition.

14. Christ and the Word: the center of intelligibility

Bonaventure’s theory of mind is Christocentric because his theory of intelligibility is centered on the Word. The Word is the expressive principle of divine wisdom and the exemplar through whom creatures are intelligible. In Christ, the Word becomes incarnate; and in Christ’s human soul Bonaventure sees human knowledge at its perfection.

This is especially clear in discussions of De scientia Christi. Recent scholarship emphasizes that Bonaventure’s illumination theory is deeply connected to the Incarnate Word: Christ’s human knowledge displays human cognition without the impediments of sin, and Bonaventure’s theory of illumination is not a vague appeal to divinity but a Christologically ordered account of how created wisdom depends on uncreated Wisdom.

This does not mean that created knowledge is swallowed up by divine knowledge. Bonaventure does not make human wisdom disappear. On the contrary, the soul needs created wisdom in order to attain uncreated wisdom. The Word grounds and perfects created cognition rather than replacing it.

For philosophy of mind, Christ functions as the perfect case of human knowing: the human soul in its fullest illumination, ordered without defect to divine truth. Bonaventure’s Christology is therefore not an external doctrine pasted onto noetics. It is part of the structure by which he thinks finite mind can be perfected.

15. The divine mind: Eternal Art, Word, truth, and love

Speaking of “the mind of God” in Bonaventure requires care. God is not a very large human mind. God is simple, uncreated, infinite, and identical with the divine act of being. Still, Bonaventure clearly speaks of divine wisdom, the Eternal Art, the Word, and the divine attributes of being, truth, and goodness. If we use “divine mind” as a cautious philosophical shorthand, it names God as the uncreated source of intelligibility, truth, exemplarity, and love.

In Itinerarium III, Bonaventure says that if God is perfect spirit, then God has memory, intelligence, and will; that the Word is begotten; that love is breathed forth; and that the distinction in God is personal rather than essential or accidental. This is a profoundly analogical claim. The human triad mirrors the divine Trinity, but the divine Trinity is not reducible to the human triad.

The divine mind has at least four roles in Bonaventure’s philosophy of mind.

First, it is the exemplar ground of created intelligibility. Things can be known because they have forms grounded in divine Wisdom.

Second, it is the normative ground of certainty. The human mind’s judgments about necessary truth are measured by immutable truth.

Third, it is the teleological ground of desire. The will’s desire for goodness ultimately points toward the highest Good.

Fourth, it is the limit and fulfillment of finite cognition. God is both that by which the mind knows and that toward which the mind is ordered, but God also exceeds the mind’s ordinary conceptual grasp.

The mind of God, then, is indispensable to Bonaventure’s philosophy of mind because it explains why created cognition can be true, why truth is stable, why desire has a final object, and why the mind’s highest fulfillment is contemplative union rather than mere information-processing.

16. Angelic minds: finite incorporeal intellects

Angelic minds occupy a crucial middle position. They are not embodied like human beings, but they are not divine. They have intellectual knowledge and will, but they do not have bodily senses. Harm Goris’s comparative study of angelic knowledge in Aquinas and Bonaventure frames the problem well: angelology forces medieval thinkers to navigate between humanizing angels too much and deifying them too much.

For Bonaventure, angels are finite created persons. Universal hylomorphism marks their finitude: even incorporeal creatures have potency and composition, whereas God alone is pure act. This makes angelic mind an important boundary case. It shows what created intellect might be without bodily sensation, but it also shows that incorporeality alone does not equal divine knowledge.

The French study of personhood in Bonaventure notes that angels and human souls are both images of God because both are rational natures immediately ordered to God. It also distinguishes their roles: angels may be superior in the governing order, but the human soul shares with them the fundamental dignity of rational imagehood.

In the Itinerarium, Bonaventure also links the soul’s internal ascent to the angelic hierarchy. The reformed soul becomes hierarchical: purged, illumined, and perfected, with internal stages corresponding to the angelic orders. Entering into oneself becomes a way of contemplating God in the angelic order.

Philosophically, angelic minds help Bonaventure separate three issues that modern discussions sometimes conflate: embodiment, finitude, and rationality. Human mind is embodied, finite, and rational. Angelic mind is incorporeal, finite, and rational. Divine mind is incorporeal, infinite, and the source of rationality. This gives Bonaventure a richer taxonomy than a simple dualism between body and mind.

17. The mind as mirror and ladder

The structure of the Itinerarium is itself a philosophy of mind. The soul ascends from the world outside itself, to the mind within itself, to God above itself. The journey has six stages, followed by a seventh stage of ecstatic union. The exterior world, the interior mind, and the superior divine reality are not unrelated domains. They are ordered mirrors.

Italian scholarship on the Itinerarium has fruitfully emphasized the images of the ladder and the mirror. Andrea Di Maio’s reading highlights the way “mirror” can signify divine wisdom, the mind, and the world: the mind mirrors the world and itself, and through this complex reflection recognizes God.

This is a helpful interpretive key. Bonaventure’s mind is not just a container of representations. It is a mirror that can be clouded, polished, oriented, and transformed. It is also a ladder, not because it physically moves upward, but because its acts can be ordered from lower to higher forms of cognition: sensation, judgment, self-knowledge, illumination, contemplation, and union.

Étienne Gilson’s French reception of Bonaventure remains important here. Gilson treats Bonaventure’s philosophy as centered around an “order of the heart” and around Christ the Word; he does not regard the mystical dimension as an embarrassment to philosophy but as part of the system’s organizing principle.

18. Reconstruction: twelve theses for a Bonaventurian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive reconstruction can be stated in twelve theses.

1. Mind is a created, finite, teleological power of living substance.
Mind is not an autonomous Cartesian ego or a computational subsystem. Human mind is the rational soul, a living principle ordered toward truth and goodness.

2. Human mind is embodied in its ordinary earthly operation.
The senses are doors through which the world enters the soul. Perception is not optional for human knowing in the present life. Bonaventure’s human knower is not an angel trapped in a body.

3. Cognition is intentional through likeness.
Objects are known through species or similitudes. These are not physical substances entering the soul, but likenesses by which the object becomes cognitively present.

4. Cognition is semiotic and participatory.
Created things are vestiges, rational creatures are images, and the mind can move from creaturely likenesses toward the divine source of likeness. Knowing is therefore not mere representation but participation in an intelligible order.

5. The created intellect is genuinely active.
The agent intellect abstracts intelligible forms; the possible intellect receives them. Bonaventure’s illumination theory does not erase the natural operations of the mind.

6. Certitude requires divine illumination.
Because the mind and created objects are mutable, certainty about necessary and immutable truth requires relation to immutable light. Illumination grounds the normativity of judgment without replacing created cognition.

7. Memory is more than recollection.
Memory includes self-presence, temporal synthesis, retention of principles, and orientation toward eternity. It is one of the deepest structures of mind.

8. Intellect presupposes Being, truth, and the Eternal Art.
Definition, proposition, and inference all depend on an intelligible order that exceeds the mutable mind. The intellect is metaphysically embedded.

9. Will and affect are constitutive of mind.
Love, desire, delight, and choice are not external to cognition. They shape attention, judgment, and the soul’s orientation toward truth.

10. Moral formation is cognitive formation.
Sin, concupiscence, phantasms, and distraction deform knowing; grace and virtue restore the mind’s capacity to see and love rightly.

11. Mystical cognition is trans-discursive, not anti-intellectual.
The highest union exceeds ordinary intellectual operation, but it follows and fulfills the prior work of perception, reason, judgment, and contemplation.

12. A full philosophy of mind must be comparative: divine, angelic, and human minds belong together.
God is the uncreated source and norm of intelligibility; angels are finite incorporeal intellects; humans are finite embodied intellects. The differences among them clarify what mind is.

19. Bonaventure and modern philosophy of mind

Bonaventure is not doing contemporary philosophy of mind, but his thought intersects with several modern problems.

Against reductive materialism, he insists that the human soul has intellectual, volitional, and spiritual operations that cannot be reduced to bodily sensation. But against a disembodied rationalism, he insists that human knowing normally begins with the senses.

Against empiricism, he argues that sensation and abstraction cannot by themselves account for necessary and certain truth. But against a crude supernaturalism, he gives the created intellect its own active powers.

Against internalism, he grounds certainty in a light beyond the finite mind. But against skepticism, he claims that the mind can really know because it is ordered to truth.

Against purely intellectualist accounts of mind, he gives will, affect, delight, love, and virtue a constitutive role in cognition.

Against a purely propositional theory of knowledge, he allows for experiential and transformative knowledge, especially through spiritual senses and mystical union.

The closest modern analogies are externalist epistemology, virtue epistemology, embodied cognition, affective intentionality, semiotics, and participatory realism. These are analogies only. Bonaventure’s own framework is metaphysical, theological, and contemplative in ways that modern categories often fail to capture.

20. Important non-English reception and interpretive emphases

Several non-English or non-Anglo strands sharpen the reconstruction.

The Spanish discussion around illumination and the agent intellect is useful because it resists an over-theologized misreading. Muñiz Rodríguez’s study emphasizes that the agent intellect is not God but a light of the human soul, while illumination remains a metaphysical doctrine. That helps preserve Bonaventure’s balance between natural intellectual activity and divine grounding.

The German discussion represented by Mladenov is valuable for the affective-volitional dimension. It highlights will, synderesis, grace, and the apex affectus, showing that Bonaventure’s epistemology cannot be reduced to intellect plus divine light. The soul’s affective orientation is part of the cognitive drama.

French scholarship is especially important for personhood, angelology, and the triadic image of God. The French study on persons in Bonaventure stresses that angels and humans are both rational images of God through memory, intelligence, and will, and that Bonaventure gives these powers a deep substantial rootedness in the soul.

The French Gilsonian tradition remains important because it refuses to separate Bonaventure’s philosophy from contemplation, Christology, and the order of love. Gilson’s reading treats Bonaventure’s thought as systematic precisely because of its spiritual and Christocentric structure, not despite it.

Italian readings of the Itinerarium that emphasize the ladder and mirror imagery help recover the work’s philosophical architecture. The mind ascends because it mirrors, and it mirrors because creation, selfhood, and divine wisdom are ordered to one another.

Recent scholarship also continues to read Bonaventure holistically. Rubio Hípola’s 2025 article describes Bonaventure’s account as integrating intellectual, affective, and volitional dimensions within a Christocentric and Trinitarian framework, with fulfillment culminating beyond pure rationality in unitive love.

21. Tensions and open interpretive problems

Several hard questions remain.

First, the precise mechanism of illumination is difficult. If God’s light grounds certainty, how exactly does it operate without becoming either direct vision of divine ideas or an unnecessary duplication of the agent intellect? The best reading is moderate: divine light grounds certitude while created cognitive powers supply content and abstraction. But the boundary between illumination, innate principles, and intellectual judgment remains debated.

Second, Bonaventure’s account of the soul’s powers differs from Thomistic alternatives. If memory, intelligence, and will are deeply intrinsic to the soul rather than merely accidental powers, then the metaphysics of faculties becomes more than a technical scholastic dispute. It affects whether mind is understood as a substance-like unity with internal articulations or as a substance plus accidental capacities.

Third, the mystical endpoint is interpretively delicate. Bonaventure says intellectual operations are left behind in the final passage, yet the journey is intellectually rigorous. The best interpretation is that discursive intellect is surpassed, not despised. The mind reaches an object too great for ordinary conceptual operation.

Fourth, angelic cognition remains underdeveloped compared with Aquinas’s more systematic angelology, but that does not make it marginal. Bonaventure’s angelology clarifies the difference between embodied finite intellect, incorporeal finite intellect, and infinite divine wisdom.

Fifth, the phrase “mind of God” must always remain analogical. Bonaventure’s God is not a psychological subject among other subjects. Still, divine memory, intelligence, will, Word, Wisdom, and Love are essential to the architecture of his account. Eliminating them would destroy the theory rather than secularize it.

22. Primary texts for a serious extraction

The most important primary text for this project is the Itinerarium mentis in Deum, especially chapters 2–4 and 7. Chapter 2 gives the account of sensory cognition, likeness, delight, and judgment. Chapter 3 gives the triadic account of memory, intelligence, and will. Chapter 4 gives reformation, spiritual senses, and the mind’s angelic-hierarchical structure. Chapter 7 gives the trans-discursive consummation.

The De scientia Christi, especially question 4, is central for illumination and the agent intellect; the later questions are important for Christ’s knowledge as the perfected case of human cognition. The Commentary on the Sentences is indispensable for detailed scholastic positions on the soul, faculties, angels, imagehood, illumination, and the agent intellect. The Breviloquium gives a compressed systematic theology of creation, soul, grace, and return. The De reductione artium ad theologiam is important for the doctrine of lights and the ordering of the sciences. The Collationes in Hexaemeron gives Bonaventure’s late synthesis of knowledge, wisdom, and contemplation. Standard bibliographies identify the Quaracchi edition and modern translations of these works as core materials for serious study.

23. Final synthesis

A Bonaventurian philosophy of mind can be stated compactly as follows.

The human mind is the rational soul as embodied image of God. It receives the sensible world through likenesses, abstracts intelligible content through its own powers, judges under the light of immutable truth, remembers itself and first principles, expresses itself in understanding, and fulfills itself in love. Its cognitive life is inseparable from its moral and affective condition: disordered love darkens knowledge, while grace and virtue restore the mind’s capacity for truth. Its highest knowing is not mere proposition but transformed participation.

The divine mind, cautiously understood, is the uncreated Eternal Art: the source of all intelligibility, the norm of all certitude, the exemplar of all forms, and the final object of all desire. Angelic minds are finite incorporeal intellects whose existence shows that rationality is not identical with embodiment, while their finitude shows that incorporeality is not divinity. Human minds occupy the middle position: embodied like material creatures, intellectual like angels, and image-bearing in relation to God.

The result is not a psychology in the modern empirical sense, nor a philosophy of mind that can be detached from metaphysics. It is a full noetic anthropology: a theory of perception, abstraction, judgment, memory, selfhood, will, affect, illumination, spiritual perception, and contemplative fulfillment. Its deepest claim is that mind is ordered participation in truth and love.

PreviousAnselm of Canterbury NextThomas Aquinas

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
